speaker: interviewer
text: [? --the ?] 2nd of November, 1995. So when you realized where they'd all gone, were you-- [INAUDIBLE] were you-- how did you feel, that you'd saved some from this death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Glad that we saved a few, but it wasn't enough. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Still today, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we see the faces of the people who left and you couldn't save, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] who went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I said about [NON-ENGLISH], that she came early in the morning and threw her arms around me and said good-bye. I see her face as she came in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a warm-blooded woman, a good wife, marvelous mother. Why did she have to be gassed? She never did anything wrong to anybody. And it was all those other people, too. What has those-- what have those children ever done to deserve to be gassed? I think you don't do that to an animal. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's something they say you have to forgive and forget. I can't. No, I can never forgive what-- what the Germans did to the Jews. Here in Australia, do you associate with Jewish-- many Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not many. There aren't many left. But we have Jewish friends in Waverley, in [? Marouba, ?] in [INAUDIBLE]. We see them. They all look the same. They ring us and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so in Holland, the Dutch government gave you a citation in 1981?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I forgot about it. It doesn't mean so much to us. We didn't do it for an medal or for anything. We helped during the war. And when the war was over, we started to think about ourselves. My husband needed another job. He couldn't go back to Indonesia, what had been his plan. So he had to be satisfied with his job in Holland. And he had quite a lot of friends in-- in-- in Holland, Jewish friends. That was strange. We hardly had any other friends than Jews. What we had gone through during the war years, that had brought you so close together. Even my relatives can't understand it. My brother is quite angry with me. Then he said, you and your Jewish friends, the so-called friends of yours. I couldn't care what he says.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why does he say so-called?

speaker: interviewee
text: He can't understand it. We never had Jewish friends, that we have got so many Jewish friends. So I think it's a form of jealousy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I noticed when we come into your house, you've got shalom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a menorah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children have got it too. We bought them. Shalom-- I think that should be in every house. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Peace all over the world. That's what we need.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should be like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have other artifacts from Israel and-- and now. How do you feel when you go back to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marvelous. I love Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I often said, if we have to choose where to live, Holland or Israel, I would like to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you keep in touch with people now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who-- who were in the camp and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, several of them died in the meantime. But [NON-ENGLISH], we still write. And when we go to Israel she's always the first to see us. [NON-ENGLISH], what my husband showed you, the in-laws, parents-in-law of [NON-ENGLISH], when we were in Tel Aviv his secretary picked us up, and she brought us to Netanya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the JNF?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the JNF.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time when we go to Israel, the JNF drives us around and brings us to several parts. We've paid there for some [? growth. ?] And we end up paying for a new water reservoir. I don't know where. The one was paid off. Now that's ready. And we are-- [? is it ?] second--

speaker: interviewer
text: The one in the Negev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: So if we are still going to Jerusalem, or to Israel, I'm sure they will show us the dams. But I don't think we will go anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think has come out of this? Is there anything positive that came out of what you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your involvement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps that the state of Israel was formed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your involvement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was only small. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You can never do enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was only a small part. And when the war was over and we were living in The Hague, well, we started to build on for ourselves. We never talked about it. And funny enough, we had a house there with balconies at the back. And we had the middle balcony, and then on both sides we had another balcony from the people upstairs. And I was washing on the balcony-- the hard way, not with a washing machine, but by brushing piece of wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my neighbor later on said, Mrs. van As, is it true? Have you been in Camp Westerbork? I said, yes, but how do you know? And she said, well, I had a friend over, and he saw you on the balcony. And he said, is that Mrs. van As? If those people hadn't been in Westerbork, Westerbork would have been totally different for us-- a lot worse. I said, so who was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were the only ones there who didn't belong to the German group and who weren't Jews. So they all knew us. But we didn't know the [? 75,000 ?] people who came through. We knew-- knew the people who were working for my husband, who were working for me, who-- for instance, the boy who came to bring us our food when we had to eat from the kitchen and people very close around us. But the others we didn't know. But they all knew us, because we were the only ones walking around without a star and not being in the German group. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So Westerbork was a better place because you were there. Was that what they were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we'll just start talking about some of the photos. First of all, could you tell me the story of this photo album?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's in-- was an Indonesian album. My husband had it. So we used that for our family photos. And this was in the camp. This was the little house we had. And later on, we had two next to each other, and that was behind the barbed wire. This was inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the one next to us was an Jewish chap. My husband may have shown you the photo for Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]. And [? Hennie ?] always called him [NON-ENGLISH], uncle. And she would say, Uncle [NON-ENGLISH], I'm here. I'm on the potty. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And what he would do then-- those rods, those-- window has a door what moves outside. And I closed it but didn't put a hook on it. So he would nip open the window and step inside, always careful that no one could see him. And [? Hennie ?] would run over to him, [NON-ENGLISH], and she had a whole story. Later on, she called her daughter after him and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you even have a dog. Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have pets there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We called him Tommy, Tommy for the English people who had to bring us the freedom. So we called him Tommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were allowed to have a dog, your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, [? mind ?] you. And one day, we had the dog already for, I think, two years, we got a message the dog wasn't from pure breed. Had to be destroyed. Well, we didn't want to destroy the dog.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we brought it to a farmer in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was about five kilometers from-- from us. And they brought it to another farm, and he brought it [INAUDIBLE], so we didn't know where it was. And after the war, he came back to us. So he was a pure breed. Hopeless people, those Germans. So we might look at some other photos in the album. Who put the album together?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband did. And Leo [NON-ENGLISH], who was in [NON-ENGLISH], he decorated it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish boy, perhaps 24 years old. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he draw a lot of-- a lot of pictures. He-- there are pictures here and two from him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Painted my children's little cots, just beautiful painting-- pictures on it. So I often had him inside, and he would talk his head off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day he said, I'm going to get married. I said, Leo, when? So I brought some butter to the kitchen and said, make me a nice cake. He didn't survive. He died of typhus, too, in Bergen-Belsen. And his wife later remarried-- Kitty. So I met her a few years ago in [NON-ENGLISH]. Could we look at some other pictures in the book?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is on those pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this inside, you see here one of the barracks, and we had a [? little ?] grass behind the house. Here were two rooms and an kind of laundry, what we had there, and a toilet. Here were two bedrooms, and those were on the [? boulevard ?] in [NON-ENGLISH]. Well, we had one chap, a gardener, and he made our windows-- our garden that way that no one could look inside our house, and we could see exactly what was going on on the other side. Because there were some [INAUDIBLE] living. You couldn't trust them. You knew. So we knew-- had to be able to see what they were doing, but no one was allowed to see what we were doing, because we often had Jews inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbits. And the children in the camp loved to see them because it was the only thing what really was living for them. So we always had a lot of children staying around the rabbits.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose rabbits were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that are the barracks again. But there were the little rooms. They had a room and a-- and sitting room, a little room behind and bedroom and a kitchen. But they weren't allowed to live as a family there. They had, for instance, four living here and four living at the back, and the eight had to do with one toilet. And that were the good units. Because in the back barracks, they had, I think, 300 living on both sides. It was a large building, and the men were on the one side, and the women were on the other side. And there were beds three high, and no one had anything for themselves. All those women were living there together, and men on the other side. And the food came from the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children? Who were they with? Were they with the parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were with the parents, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were these photos taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: By Breslauer. He had an [NON-ENGLISH] small studio. And we went over there, and he took them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he allowed to take photos of your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a photographer, and they had given him some room. And he made films in the camp, and he made photos in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who wanted it documented?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gemmeker. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll tell you-- it was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder why.

speaker: interviewee
text: --strange, strange business being there. Because it was a concentration camp, and they had cabaret, and they had orchestras. And what I said, one day we got a [NON-ENGLISH], and they made a little place, and they could have coffee and biscuits there-- the Jewish could. And for the [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], they needed special money, and there was special money printed. You could only buy it with [NON-ENGLISH] money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling me another story about another photo album that you found after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean that of the cabaret?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: You might just mention that now while we're looking at the photo album.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was ended. And Professor [NON-ENGLISH] asked me, Mrs. van As, will you do me a pleasure? Go with me to the magazine, to that big building. And when I came there, he said, take what you like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, OK. Are the Jews allowed to do it, too? He said, no. I said, well, then I'm not. So I only took out an album what I knew would be good to keep for people after us. And I took that out, and that album is now in Yad Vashem. What's in the album?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole cabaret, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the scenes they played in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So people can have an idea what happened there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this-- these photos here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this bin was drawn by Leo [NON-ENGLISH], and [? Hennie ?] is next to it wondering what's coming there on the other side when Nelly was born. And this bin was shown all over the camp. On it said [NON-ENGLISH]. That means only for food [? wastes. ?] And this-- the food from these tins, I think it were 124 pigs were fed in the-- in the farm, near the farm. You could find them all over the camp. So that show you that people didn't have to go hungry. There were-- was food-- food enough. And that was thanks to my husband, who always was writing more out than they were entitled to. But that was a thing he could do. Sick persons got more, and expecting women got more. And-- and no one was controlling if there were 100 sick people or 150. So he had a chance there to do more than-- than he was allowed to, and that gave the people a lot more chance to have what they needed inside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these photos were also taken inside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're all taken by Breslauer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was a very good photographer. And he always asked us when he had the film. He loved the kids. He would say, I have a film. Come over, and I'll make you some photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's lovely. All [? right. ?] And this is the medal from the Dutch government, is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's from the Dutch government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does the citation say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Resistance Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Resistance Cross. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a sign that you resisted the-- the Germans. And for the resistment-- resistance, we got this medal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the tyrants getting out of your country. Send them off, the tyrants.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the medal--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were tyrants, no doubt about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole citation, what does it actually say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think it says very much to you. [NON-ENGLISH]. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: In English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't even translate it all. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: But it is-- it's the resistance medal for getting--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for banishing--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it says--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the tyrants.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [NON-ENGLISH]. So that's [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Remembrance Cross for the resistance we showed in those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Yes, and this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one was taken of our family when we got the medal rewarded that evening in the Masonic hall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- the-- the medal we've just been speaking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's right. That's the Yad Vashem medal.

speaker: interviewer
text: We haven't--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our eldest daughter, second daughter, son, and their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think we'd better look at the Yad Vashem medal next. These two medals are from Israel. Could you tell us about them, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is from Jerusalem itself. But this one, what was presented to us here in Sydney, is the Yad Vashem medal that was given to us for the help we gave to Jewish people during the war. And although we never did it to get a medal, we are still proud to have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read to us what it says on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What does it say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe this is where--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe-- maybe Mr. van As should come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do we want [? a seat? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we filming now? Now, Mr. van As, perhaps you could explain what's on the medals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on this medal it says our names first. And then underneath it says, 'The People of'-- 'The Jewish People Remember.' And on the side it says, 'He who has saved one life, has saved them all.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And this certificate? Certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my husband's, and I would like him to tell you exactly what's on this Certificate of Honor.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'This is to certify that in its session of December 2, 1991, the Commission for the Designation of the Righteous, established by Yad Vashem, the Holocaust Heroes, and Martyrs' Remembrance Authority, on the basis of evidence presented before it, has decided to honor Adrianus and Bertha van As-Sorensen, who, during the Holocaust period in Europe, risked their lives to save persecuted Jews. The Commission therefore has accorded them the Medal of Righteous Among the Nations. Their names shall be forever engraved on the Honor Wall in the Garden of the Righteous at Yad Vashem, Jerusalem.' And it was issued on March the 4th, 1992.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And we have a photo of that, haven't we? And could you just tell me what these-- these photos are, Mr. van As?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- the wall in Jerusalem where the-- that plaque was unveiled by us on-- in May 1992 when we were in Jerusalem. And that's a-- a representative of the Jewish National Fund who was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, this is [? Hennie, ?] who was then year and a half when we came to Camp Westerbork-- she's the only one who still remembers a few things-- her husband, her son, and two daughters. And this daughter got married that day, Sharon, who's called after two Jewish friends of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Christopher, Belinda, and Sharon. That are her three. And here is [? Hennie ?] again with Sharon, her daughter, and her two grandchildren-- identical twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Minerva and Monopoly. So that our first great-grandchildren. This here is Nelly, our second daughter. She was born in July '43. So in the camp they all knew about her birth and when she was an little toddler. Her husband, her daughter Kim, and her sons Andrew and Jason. That was on the day Kim was married to Chris. This here is our son. He was born July '45. So he was the child the whole camp was waiting for, and it was our [INAUDIBLE] child. His son, Timothy, and those four children are adopted-- Erica, Philip, Debby, and Leonie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his wife [? Anne. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: In conclusion, maybe you could--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, may I introduce my husband, Adrianus van As. And we have been together since--

speaker: interviewee
text: '37. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's '37, so a very long time. It's nearly 60 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And still? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I do it, to survive that long, eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: You survived worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went through together, and now here you are in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep, yep. We went to Australia together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were just--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through the camp time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thereafter, we went to The Hague.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lived there for nine years. And after nine years, we came to Australia, are quite happy here.

speaker: interviewee
text: 40 years now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next month.

speaker: interviewee
text: This month, this month.

speaker: interviewee
text: This month-- this month's 40 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There have been several times to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And enjoyed very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Indeed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So yeah, end of the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you wouldn't change it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm-mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, till the [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]
